title: Roman Hispania
subtitle: 27 BC–409 AD · Emperors, Philosophers and Christianisation

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From Augustus's provinces to the emperors born in Hispania

entries.label: Augustus reorganises Hispania — three provinces
entries.hint: Augustus reorganises Hispania into three provinces: Tarraconensis (senatorial-turned-imperial), Baetica (senatorial) and Lusitania (imperial); Emerita Augusta (Mérida) is founded as the capital of Lusitania in 25 BC for veteran soldiers; the reorganisation creates the administrative framework for four centuries.
entries.note: Cassius Dio LIII.12 and 26: primary sources for Augustus's reorganisation. The founding of Emerita Augusta 25 BC: Cassius Dio LIII.26 (primary). The Res Gestae Divi Augusti (Augustus's own account): documents Hispania's place in the empire. The three-province structure: documented in administrative inscriptions throughout Hispania.

entries.label: Trajan and Hadrian — emperors born at Italica
entries.loc: Italica (Santiponce, near Seville) — birthplace of both
entries.hint: Trajan (emperor 98–117 AD) is born at Italica near Seville; Hadrian's birthplace is contested — Cassius Dio (69.1) says Italica, the Historia Augusta (Vita Hadriani 1.3) says Rome; most modern scholars favour Italica given his family's deep roots there; under Hadrian, Italica is rebuilt as a major imperial city with lead water pipes stamped with his name surviving in situ.
entries.note: Trajan's Italica origin: Historia Augusta, Vita Traiani 1.1 (uncontested). Hadrian's birthplace: Cassius Dio 69.1 says Italica; Historia Augusta Vita Hadriani 1.3 says 'Natus est Romae' — a genuine scholarly dispute. Most modern scholars favour Italica: Birley (1997), Hadrian: The Restless Emperor, ch.1. Lead pipes stamped 'IMP HADRIANI': in situ at Italica (UNESCO tentative list documentation). The amphitheatre: one of the largest in the empire.

entries.label: Theodosius — the last great emperor, born in Hispania
entries.hint: Theodosius I (the Great) is most likely born at Cauca in Hispania — Hydatius and Zosimus name Cauca; Marcellinus Comes argues for Italica; his family's deep roots in Baetica are not disputed; he becomes the last emperor to rule both halves of the Roman Empire.
entries.note: Theodosius's Cauca origin: Zosimus IV.24 and Hydatius, Chronicle (primary). Marcellinus Comes attributes Italica — a minority view. Birley (2009) on the birthplace debate. The Edict of Thessalonica 380 AD (Cunctos populos): Codex Theodosianus XVI.1.2 — mandates Nicene Christianity specifically against Arianism; paganism not outlawed until separate decrees of 391–392 AD. His death 17 Jan 395: documented.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Sertorian War, Roman civil wars in Hispania and the Germanic crossing

entries.label: Cantabrian Wars — the opening act of Roman Hispania
entries.hint: The Cantabrian Wars are not yet fully concluded at Augustus's reorganisation of 27 BC — resistance continues until 19 BC when Agrippa finally pacifies the north; the wars directly drive the foundation of Emerita Augusta (25 BC) for veterans and Caesaraugusta (14 BC); Augustus's three-province reorganisation is military administration of a territory still being conquered.
entries.note: Cassius Dio LIII.25–26: Augustus's personal command. Agrippa's final campaign 19 BC: Cassius Dio LIV.5. The foundation of Emerita Augusta: explicitly for veterans of the Cantabrian Wars — Cassius Dio LIII.26. See hispania-300bc-27bc folio for the full Cantabrian Wars narrative.

entries.label: The Rhine crossing — Vandals and Suebi reach Hispania
entries.hint: On 31 December 406 the Rhine freezes and Vandals, Alans and Suebi pour across — by 409 they reach Hispania; the Suebi settle in Galicia, the Vandals move through Baetica toward North Africa; the popular etymology connecting 'Andalusia' to 'Vandalusia' is contested by most modern linguists, who favour other origins including Visigothic or pre-Roman toponyms.
entries.note: The Rhine crossing 31 Dec 406: Jerome, Letter 123.16 — eyewitness account ('all Gaul burned with one fire'). Prosper of Aquitaine, Chronicle: records the crossing's date. The Vandals in Baetica: Hydatius, Chronicle (contemporary Galician bishop) — the primary source for events in Hispania 406–469. The Suebi in Galicia: Hydatius covers this in detail. The name 'Andalusia' from 'Vandalusia': documented in Arabic sources.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Roman municipal law, the Flavian privilege and Christianisation edicts

entries.label: Constitutio Antoniniana — full citizenship for all Hispani
entries.hint: Caracalla's Edict of 212 AD grants full Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the empire — the logical conclusion of Vespasian's 73 AD Latin rights grant; every free person in Hispania becomes a full Roman citizen overnight; the most significant single legal change in the peninsula since the Roman conquest.
entries.note: Papyrus Giessen 40 (P.Giss.40): the primary documentary evidence for the Constitutio Antoniniana — Greek papyrus found in Egypt preserving the edict's text. Cassius Dio LXXVII.9: the literary source, noting fiscal motivation. Millar (1977), The Emperor in the Roman World: the standard analysis of the edict's significance. The practical effect in Hispania: documented through the explosion of Latin names in 3rd-century Hispanian inscriptions (EDCS database).

entries.label: Council of Elvira — the first Western church council
entries.hint: The Council of Elvira (c.306 AD) is the earliest known church council in the Western Mediterranean — 19 bishops and 26 priests attend; its 81 canons cover sexual conduct, idolatry, and Christian-pagan interaction; it is the primary documentary source for Christianity in pre-Constantinian Hispania.
entries.note: The 81 canons of Elvira: preserved in canonical collections; Laeuchli (1972), Power and Sexuality: The Emergence of Canon Law at the Synod of Elvira: the standard English study. The council's date: debated (306 vs 313) — most scholars favour c.306. The bishop list: 19 bishops from across Hispania — documents the geographical extent of early Iberian Christianity. Canon 36 (on images): the earliest documented prohibition of images in a church.

entries.label: Flavian municipal law — universal Latin rights in Hispania
entries.hint: Vespasian grants the entire Iberian Peninsula the ius Latii (Latin rights) — the single largest extension of Roman citizenship rights in history to that point; it transforms Hispania's population from provincials to Latin citizens with a path to full Roman citizenship.
entries.note: Pliny, Natural History III.30: primary source — 'Vespasian Augustus, in the storms of the state, gave Latin rights to the whole of Spain'. The Lex Irnitana (from the previous folio, c.91 AD): documents the practical implementation of Flavian municipal law. Millar (1977), The Emperor in the Roman World: contextualises the grant's significance. The Lex Malacitana (another Flavian municipal charter): BNE — parallel document.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: The martyrs of Hispania and the Christianisation of a province

entries.label: Hosius of Corduba — architect of the Nicene Creed
entries.hint: Hosius of Corduba is Constantine's personal theological advisor, presides over the Council of Elvira (c.306), chairs the Council of Nicaea (325) where the Nicene Creed is drafted, and remains active into extreme old age; no single Hispanian figure has greater influence on the institutional shape of Western Christianity.
entries.note: Hosius's role at Nicaea: Athanasius, De Synodis 43 and Historia Arianorum 42 — primary sources identifying him as the president. His role at Elvira: documented in the council acts (the bishop list). His correspondence with Constantine: partially preserved in Eusebius, Life of Constantine. Sotomayor (1979), La iglesia en la España romana y visigoda: the standard study. Hosius signed the Nicene Creed first — his signature heads the list of bishops, documented.

entries.label: Eulalia of Mérida — the most celebrated Hispanian martyr
entries.hint: Eulalia of Mérida, a 12-year-old girl, is executed during Diocletian's persecution in 304 AD — she becomes the most venerated martyr in Hispania; the poet Prudentius writes her passion (c.400); her basilica at Mérida is documented archaeologically; her cult survives through the Visigothic period.
entries.note: Prudentius, Peristephanon III (c.400 AD): the primary literary account — written within living memory of the martyrdom. The Eulalia basilica at Mérida: documented archaeologically (Mateos Cruz 1999). The Visigothic continuation of her cult: documented in the Vitas Sanctorum Patrum Emeritensium (7th c.). The 304 date: fits Diocletian's persecution decree of that year — documented.

entries.label: Priscillian — heresy, execution and the first Christian to be executed by the state
entries.hint: Priscillian, bishop of Ávila, is executed in Trier in 385 AD by the usurper Magnus Maximus — formally on a charge of maleficium (sorcery/witchcraft), not heresy; the theological motivations are clear but the legal mechanism is sorcery; condemned by Ambrose and Martin of Tours; sets the precedent for state religious persecution.
entries.note: Sulpicius Severus, Chronica II.46–51: primary source — contemporary and hostile. The formal charge: maleficium (sorcery) — not heresy, which did not yet carry the death penalty in Roman law. Ambrose's condemnation: his correspondence. Martin of Tours's opposition: Sulpicius Severus, Life of Martin. Chadwick (1976), Priscillian of Avila: the standard study — ch.7 on the legal charges. The precedent: the first execution by a Christian ruler of a Christian on religious grounds, whatever the formal charge.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: The Roman monuments of Hispania — Mérida, Segovia, Alcántara

entries.label: Theatre of Emerita Augusta — the most complete Roman theatre in Hispania
entries.loc: Emerita Augusta (mod. Mérida, Badajoz) — extant, UNESCO
entries.hint: The Roman theatre of Mérida (16–15 BC) is the best-preserved Roman theatre in Iberia — built by Agrippa, seating 6,000, with a two-storey stage backdrop still standing; still used for the annual Festival de Teatro Clásico; the dedication inscription survives in situ.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage (1993). The dedication inscription: in situ — 'M. Agrippa L.F. cos. III trib. pot. III'. The inscription dates the theatre to 16–15 BC with certainty. Álvarez Martínez (1982), El teatro romano de Mérida: the standard archaeological study. Mérida (Emerita Augusta): the best-preserved Roman city in Iberia — also contains amphitheatre, circus, bridge, arch and aqueduct.

entries.label: Alcántara bridge — the most perfect Roman bridge
entries.hint: The Trajan-era bridge at Alcántara spans the Tagus at 71 metres height — its dedicatory inscription survives: 'Pontis perfectionem perpetuam' (to the eternal perfection of this bridge); its architect Lacer dedicates a small temple to the bridge's own immortality; it is the most celebrated Roman bridge in Iberia.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Dedicatory inscription: in situ on the bridge's triumphal arch — records Trajan's reign and the architect Lacer's name. The inscription text: 'Pontem perpetui mansurum in saecula mundi' (a bridge to last through the ages of the world). Blázquez (1965) on Roman bridges in Hispania. The bridge: extant (partially reconstructed after civil war damage). The small temple to the bridge: also extant at one end.

entries.label: Prudentius — the greatest Christian Latin poet of late antiquity
entries.hint: Aurelius Prudentius Clemens, born in Tarraconensis in 348 AD, is the greatest Christian Latin poet of late antiquity — his Psychomachia (the allegorical battle of Virtues against Vices for the human soul) is the first major allegorical epic in Western literature and one of the most copied and illustrated texts of the entire Middle Ages.
entries.note: Psychomachia (c.405 AD): over 300 medieval manuscript copies survive — more than almost any other late antique text. BnF and British Library hold major illuminated manuscripts (the Psychomachia's battle scenes are the source of countless medieval allegorical illustrations). Prudentius's autobiographical poem Praefatio: documents his Tarraconensis birth and career. Mastrangelo (2008), The Roman Self in Late Antiquity: the standard modern study. His Peristephanon (hymns to martyrs): primary source for the Hispanian martyrs including Eulalia of Mérida and Vincent of Zaragoza.

entries.label: Segovia aqueduct — Roman engineering without mortar
entries.hint: The Segovia aqueduct stands 28 metres tall, has 167 arches over 818 metres, and is built entirely without mortar — the precisely cut granite blocks hold each other by friction alone; in continuous use for nearly 2,000 years, it still carried water to Segovia into the 20th century.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage (1985). The 'no mortar' construction: confirmed by modern engineering surveys. Dating: numismatic evidence suggests 1st or early 2nd c. AD — no direct inscription survives. The aqueduct's continuous use to c.1970s: documented in Spanish municipal water records. Bonnin & Raya (2005), El acueducto de Segovia: the standard technical study. The 20,400 individual granite blocks: documented.

entries.label: Roman circus of Mérida — chariot racing and spectacle
entries.hint: Emerita Augusta's circus (hippodrome) is the longest in Roman Hispania — 430 metres, seating up to 30,000; the circus, theatre and amphitheatre together make Mérida the most complete Roman entertainment complex surviving in Iberia; the circus's spina (central barrier) is documented archaeologically.
entries.note: The Mérida circus: documented in the Conjunto Arqueológico de Mérida. Sánchez-Palencia et al. (2001), El circo romano de Emerita Augusta: the standard study. The seating capacity (30,000): estimated from dimensions. Chariot racing as the primary public spectacle: documented in Roman literary sources throughout the imperial period. The circus's alignment with the street grid: documented in the archaeological plan of Mérida.

entries.label: Villa La Olmeda mosaics — Hispano-Roman elite expression
entries.loc: La Olmeda (Saldaña, Palencia) — extant, UNESCO tentative list
entries.hint: The Villa La Olmeda in Palencia contains the finest surviving floor mosaics of Roman Hispania — a 1,000 sq metre mosaic floor depicting Achilles on Skyros, hunting scenes, and geometric patterns; discovered 1968; shows how Hispano-Roman elite culture adopted and localised classical mythology in the late imperial period.
entries.note: Discovery: 1968, by landowner Javier García Guinea — complete excavation report García Guinea (1975). The Achilles-on-Skyros mosaic: the primary piece, 15×10m. Extant and open to visitors. Schlunk & Hauschild (1978), Hispania Antiqua: contextualises within Roman Hispanian villa culture. UNESCO tentative list (2007). The late 4th-century date: confirmed by coin finds in the mosaic context. The Villa of Carranque (Toledo): parallel site with Orpheus mosaics — also extant.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Hispania's remarkable literary generation — Seneca, Lucan, Quintilian, Martial

entries.label: Seneca — the Stoic philosopher from Córdoba
entries.hint: Lucius Annaeus Seneca is born in Córdoba and becomes the most influential philosopher of the Roman imperial period — his Letters, Tragedies and Moral Essays are the primary documents of Stoic thought in Latin; he is Nero's tutor and is forced to commit suicide on Nero's orders in 65 AD.
entries.note: Seneca's birth in Corduba: Seneca, Consolatio ad Helviam 17.3 (autobiographical reference). His death: Tacitus, Annals XV.60–64 (primary, detailed, near-contemporary). The Letters to Lucilius: 124 letters — BnF and Bodleian hold medieval manuscripts. The tragedies: first printed edition Ferrara 1484. Wilson (2014), The Greatest Empire: A Life of Seneca: the standard modern biography.

entries.label: Quintilian — the rhetoric teacher from Calagurris
entries.loc: Calagurris (mod. Calahorra, La Rioja) — born; Rome (career)
entries.hint: Marcus Fabius Quintilianus is born in Calagurris and becomes Rome's first state-funded professor of rhetoric — his Institutio Oratoria (12 books) is the most comprehensive ancient treatise on education and rhetoric; its recovery by Poggio Bracciolini in 1416 is a landmark of the Renaissance.
entries.note: Institutio Oratoria (c.95 AD): 12 books. The full text recovered by Poggio Bracciolini at the monastery of Saint Gall 1416: documented in Poggio's letters. BnF and British Library hold early manuscripts. Quintilian's state salary: Suetonius, De Grammaticis et Rhetoribus 17 — 'Vespasian was the first to give rhetoric teachers an annual salary of 100,000 sesterces from the treasury'. Kennedy (1969), Quintilian: the standard English study.

entries.label: Martial — the epigrammatist from Bilbilis
entries.loc: Bilbilis (mod. Calatayud, Zaragoza) — born; Rome (career); Bilbilis (retired)
entries.hint: Marcus Valerius Martialis is born in Bilbilis and writes the 12 books of Epigrams that are the primary documentary source for daily life in 1st-century Rome — Latin literature's most direct social observation; he retires to Bilbilis in old age and his later poems document small-town Hispanian life.
entries.note: Martial's Bilbilis origin: Martial, Epigrams X.103 and XII.18 (self-documented). The Epigrams (c.86–102 AD): 12 books; first printed edition Venice 1501. The retirement to Bilbilis: Epigrams XII.18. Sullivan (1991), Martial: the Unexpected Classic: the standard English study. Martial's social documentation: he names over 1,000 individuals — the richest social record in Latin poetry.

entries.label: Lucan — the epic poet who died for Calpurnius Piso's conspiracy
entries.hint: Marcus Annaeus Lucanus (Lucan) is Seneca's nephew, born in Córdoba — his Bellum Civile (Pharsalia) is the greatest Latin epic after the Aeneid, covering Caesar's civil war without gods; he joins the Pisonian conspiracy against Nero and is forced to commit suicide at 25.
entries.note: Lucan's Córdoba birth: Suetonius, De Poetis (Vita Lucani). The Bellum Civile (Pharsalia): 10 books — earliest manuscripts 9th c., BnF and Monte Cassino. His death 65 AD: Tacitus, Annals XV.56 and 70 (primary). Fantham (1992), Lucan: De Bello Civili Book II (Cambridge): the standard modern commentary. The Bellum Civile's Republican ideology: the reason Nero banned it and the reason it survives — it circulated underground.

entries.label: Orosius — the first Christian philosophy of history
entries.hint: Paulus Orosius, a native of Gallaecia, writes Historiarum Adversum Paganos Libri VII (Seven Books of History Against the Pagans, c.416–418) at Augustine's request — the first systematic Christian philosophy of history arguing that all empires rise and fall under divine providence toward a unified Christian world order; it becomes the fundamental framework for medieval historical and political thought.
entries.note: Historiarum Adversum Paganos (c.416–418): written at the request of Augustine of Hippo — Augustine, City of God XVIII.52 explicitly mentions Orosius. Multiple manuscript traditions; critical edition Zangemeister (1882), CSEL 5. English translation Deferrari (1964), Fathers of the Church. Orosius's Gallaecian origin: documented in his own text (Hist. I.2.1). Arnaud-Lindet (1990–1991), Orose: the standard modern French edition. The text's influence: used as the primary historical framework in medieval education for 800 years — documented in monastic library catalogues.

entries.label: Priscillianism — ascetic theology and gender equality in the church
entries.hint: Priscillian's theological school — condemned at Zaragoza (380) and leading to his execution (385) — advocates rigorous asceticism, apocryphal scriptural study, vegetarianism, rejection of episcopal hierarchy, and active equality of women in theological study; the movement persists in northwestern Spain for two centuries despite condemnation, documented in ongoing Council acts.
entries.note: The Würzburg Tractates: 11 Priscillianist texts discovered 1885 — Universitätsbibliothek Würzburg, Mp.th.q.3; the only surviving Priscillianist writings. Schepss (1889), Priscilliani quae supersunt: the editio princeps. The Council of Zaragoza 380 (condemning): acts preserved in the Collectio Hispana. Chadwick (1976), Priscillian of Avila: the standard study. The movement's persistence: documented in the Councils of Toledo (1st through 4th) which keep condemning it — indicating ongoing presence.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Baetican olive oil, Hispanian silver and the peninsula's wealth

entries.label: Las Médulas — the Roman Empire's largest gold mine
entries.hint: Las Médulas in León is the largest open-cast gold mine in the entire Roman Empire — the Romans use ruina montium (wrecking of mountains), a hydraulic mining technique that collapses entire hillsides with pressurised water; an estimated 5 million tonnes of gold ore extracted over 250 years; the dramatic landscape of red cliffs and caves survives today.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage (1997). Pliny the Elder, Natural History XXXIII.66–78: the primary ancient description of ruina montium — Pliny served in Hispania as a procurator and observed the mines firsthand. The estimated gold output: Domergue (1990), Les mines de la péninsule ibérique Vol.I. The landscape today: the most dramatic archaeological landscape in Roman Iberia. Sánchez-Palencia et al. (2000), Las Médulas (León): the standard modern archaeological study.

entries.label: Roman Hispania at its peak — three emperors, five writers
entries.loc: Italica; Corduba; Calagurris; Bilbilis; Emerita Augusta
entries.hint: The period 70–200 AD is Hispania's cultural and political peak — three emperors (Trajan, Hadrian, Theodosius) and five of Latin literature's greatest writers (Seneca, Lucan, Martial, Quintilian, and the geographer Pomponius Mela) all come from the peninsula; no other province contributes more to the Roman imperial project.
entries.note: The concentration of Hispanian talent: noted by ancient writers including Juvenal (who mocks Iberian poets flooding Rome). The five writers: all documented as Iberian-born in ancient sources. The three emperors: all documented as Iberian-born in ancient sources. The irony: Hispania, conquered so brutally by Rome, becomes one of Rome's most productive provinces — a primary case study in romanisation.

entries.label: Dressel 20 amphoras — Baetican oil across the empire
entries.hint: Baetican olive oil travels the empire in Dressel 20 amphoras — but the trade is not simply commercial; it is structurally embedded in the Annona, Rome's state-controlled supply system for the capital and legions; the amphora stamps (tituli picti) are fiscal and administrative receipts of a command economy, not mere merchant marks.
entries.note: Dressel 20 amphoras: the single most distributed amphora type in the Roman world. Monte Testaccio: Rodríguez Almeida (1984) — estimated 53 million amphoras. The stamped handles (tituli picti): carry producer names, dates, weights and inspector marks — the most detailed ancient trade documentation. Mattingly (1988), Oil for Export?: the standard study. Blázquez (1978), Economía de la Hispania romana: the broader economic context. The Annona system: Rickman (1980), The Corn Supply of Ancient Rome: the standard study — documents how Baetican oil was requisitioned for the state dole. The Monte Testaccio tituli picti: Rodriguez Almeida (1984) — each stamp records producer, inspector, weight and date (a fiscal document). The command economy framing: Garnsey & Saller (1987), The Roman Empire ch.4.

entries.label: Annona command economy — Monte Testaccio as fiscal archive
entries.hint: Baetican olive oil production is not simple commercial trade — it is structurally bound to Rome's Annona, the state-controlled dole supplying the capital and the legions; the amphora stamps at Monte Testaccio are fiscal and administrative receipts of a highly regulated imperial command economy, not mere merchant marks; the mountain of 53 million amphoras is Rome's largest surviving fiscal archive.
entries.note: Monte Testaccio tituli picti: Rodriguez Almeida (1984) — the primary publication of the stamp corpus. Remesal Rodríguez (1986), La Annona militaris y la exportación de aceite bético: the standard study of the Annona-Baetica connection. The stamps record: producer name, date of filling, weight of oil, inspector's name, and receiving official — a complete fiscal transaction record. Mattingly (1988): contextualises Baetica within the Annona system. The command-economy framing: explicitly argued by Remesal.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Trade networks, Stoic philosophy across the empire and the Atlantic route

entries.label: Garum — the fermented fish sauce that conquered Rome
entries.loc: Baelo Claudia (mod. Bolonia, Cádiz); Carthago Nova; Baetica coast
entries.hint: Hispania is the primary producer of garum (fermented fish sauce) for the Roman Empire — factories at Baelo Claudia, Carthago Nova and along the Atlantic coast; the garum of Hispania is considered the finest; its factories are documented archaeologically as the largest food-processing sites in Roman Hispania.
entries.note: Baelo Claudia (extant, Tarifa area): UNESCO — the best-preserved garum factory site in the Roman world, fully excavated. The Scomber fish-sauce amphoras (Dressel 7–11): found across the empire. Curtis (1991), Garum and Salsamenta: the standard study. Ponsich & Tarradell (1965): the foundational archaeological survey of garum factories. Pliny, Natural History XXXI.94: documents Hispanian garum's excellence.

entries.label: Via Augusta — the logistical spine of Roman Hispania
entries.loc: Cádiz to the Pyrenees (1,500+ km road; multiple milestones extant)
entries.hint: The Via Augusta spans from Cádiz to the Pyrenees (where it joins the Via Domitia to Rome) — the logistical backbone of Roman Hispania enabling swift movement of troops, raw materials, the imperial post (cursus publicus), and the Baetican oil that feeds the Annona; its milestones, bridges and mansiones are the most numerous surviving Roman infrastructure evidence on the peninsula.
entries.note: Via Augusta milestones: over 200 surviving milestones across Spain — EDCS database. The road's construction: begun under Augustus, documented in his Res Gestae and in milestone inscriptions. The Ponte de las Alcantarillas (extant bridge): one of many Roman bridges on the route. Sillières (1990), Les voies de communication de l'Hispanie méridionale: the standard study. The cursus publicus (imperial postal relay): Codex Theodosianus VIII.5 — documents how the road system functioned as an administrative nervous system.

entries.label: Via Augusta — the logistical backbone of Roman Hispania
entries.loc: Cádiz to the Pyrenees (and connection to Via Domitia to Rome)
entries.hint: The Via Augusta runs from Cádiz through Córdoba, Cartagena and Valencia to the Pyrenees — connecting to the Via Domitia and the road to Rome; it is the logistical backbone of the entire peninsula, enabling the swift movement of troops, silver from the mines, olive oil from Baetica, and the imperial postal service (cursus publicus) that holds the empire together.
entries.note: The Via Augusta's milestones: over 100 surviving Roman milestone inscriptions along its route — documented in the EDCS database. The road's Augustan completion: CIL II.4701 (Tarragona milestone). Silliéres (1990), Les voies de communication de l'Hispanie méridionale: the standard study. The cursus publicus on the Via Augusta: documented in imperial rescripts (Digest). The physical remains: stretches surviving near Córdoba, Tarragona, and Sagunto — documented archaeologically.

entries.label: Stoic philosophy — the Senecan school and its transmission
entries.loc: Corduba (origin); Rome; later — all of Europe
entries.hint: Seneca's Stoicism, rooted in his Corduban upbringing, becomes the dominant philosophical influence on Western Europe through the Middle Ages and Renaissance — his Letters to Lucilius are the primary vector; Erasmus, Montaigne and Francis Bacon all cite Seneca; his Hispanian origin is a thread connecting ancient and early modern Europe.
entries.note: Seneca's influence on Erasmus: documented in Erasmus's edition of Seneca (1515, 1529). Montaigne's citations of Seneca: throughout the Essays — over 100 references. The manuscript transmission: Reynolds (1965), The Medieval Tradition of Seneca's Letters: the standard study of manuscript history. The Corduba connection: Seneca explicitly describes his Iberian upbringing — Consolatio ad Helviam 17.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Roman Hispania in the context of the empire's rise and fall

entries.label: Hispania's contribution to the Roman empire — and its end
entries.hint: Roman Hispania's 436 years of provincial existence is a story of remarkable integration — from resistance to the peninsula that supplied Rome with its greatest emperors and writers; the irony that the Germanic peoples who end Roman Hispania (Vandals, Suebi) enter the peninsula from the same direction as the Carthaginians and Romans before them.
entries.note: The 436-year span (27 BC–409 AD): documented in continuous administrative records. Hydatius, Chronicle (contemporary, from Galicia): the primary source for the 409 AD crossing and its immediate consequences. The Vandal name and 'Andalusia': documented in Arabic sources as 'al-Andalus' from 'Vandalusia' — though some scholars dispute this etymology. Collins (2004), Visigothic Spain: contextualises the transition.

entries.label: Crisis of the Third Century — Franks sack Tarraco
entries.hint: During the Crisis of the Third Century, Germanic Franks breach the Pyrenees and sack Tarraco (c.260 AD) — the first time a foreign enemy reaches deep into Hispania since the Punic Wars; the peninsula is temporarily absorbed into the breakaway Gallic Empire (260–274 AD) before Aurelian restores it to Roman control.
entries.note: The Frankish sack of Tarraco c.260 AD: documented in the Historia Augusta and confirmed archaeologically — destruction layers in Tarraco excavations. The Gallic Empire (260–274 AD): Hispania under Postumus and successors — documented in coinage (Gallic imperial coin series found in Hispania). Arce (2005), Bárbaros y romanos en Hispania: the standard study of the period. The crisis's long-term impact: Tarraco never recovers its former prominence.

entries.label: Legio VII Gemina — the Pax Romana's single guardian
entries.loc: León (Legio VII Gemina's permanent base; city named after it)
entries.hint: By the 1st century AD the entire Iberian Peninsula is garrisoned by a single legion — Legio VII Gemina, based at what becomes the city of León; the demilitarisation is the most concrete index of Hispania's geopolitical integration as a secure core province; a frontier still needed multiple legions, Hispania needs one.
entries.note: Legio VII Gemina's base at León: documented in Tacitus and in military diplomas (EDCS). The single-legion garrison: confirmed in all administrative sources from the Flavian period. The city of León (Legio > León): direct toponymic evidence of the legion's permanence. Roldán Hervás (1974), Hispania y el ejército romano: the standard study. The contrast: Britain needed 3–4 legions; the Rhine frontier 8 legions; Hispania 1 — the most dramatic evidence for how pacified the province was.

entries.label: Pax Romana — one legion for the whole peninsula
entries.loc: Legio VII Gemina's base: Legio (mod. León)
entries.hint: By the 1st century AD the entire Iberian peninsula is garrisoned by a single legion — the Legio VII Gemina, stationed at Legio (modern León); this near-total demilitarisation is the truest measure of Hispania's geopolitical integration; a region that required multiple legions and 200 years to subdue now needs one; it is the Pax Romana made concrete.
entries.note: Legio VII Gemina's presence in León: CIL II.2664 and multiple inscriptions (EDCS database). The legion's role: documented as construction as much as military — building the city walls of León and roads. Le Roux (1982), L'Armée romaine et l'organisation des provinces ibériques: the standard study of Roman military demilitarisation. The contrast with the conquest period (4–7 legions): documented in Livy and Appian. The single legion remains in Hispania until the 5th century collapse.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Infames — gladiators, actors and chariot racers outside the law
entries.loc: Emerita Augusta; Italica; Caesaraugusta (amphitheatres and theatres)
entries.hint: The performers who animated Hispania's theatres, amphitheatres and circuses — gladiators, charioteers, actors — were legally classified as infames; they lacked basic citizen rights and had no legal protection over their own bodies, yet were simultaneously idolised; a victorious gladiator from Emerita could be the most famous person in the province while having no standing in court.
entries.note: The infamia classification: Roman law — Digest 3.2 (Ulpian on infames). Gladiatorial contracts (auctoramenti): survived in papyri — FIRA III no.141. Hispanian gladiatorial inscriptions: EDCS database documents numerous epitaphs of gladiators, some with images. Junkelmann (2000), Das Spiel mit dem Tod: the standard study of Roman gladiatorial life. The paradox of infamia: documented in Tertullian's critique (De Spectaculis) — crowds worship those the law treats as dishonoured.

entries.label: Columella — Roman agricultural slavery documented from Hispania
entries.loc: Gades (mod. Cádiz) — born; Hispania Baetica (family estate)
entries.hint: Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella is born in Cádiz and writes the most comprehensive Roman agricultural manual (De Re Rustica, 12 books) — it documents in detail the management of enslaved agricultural workers on Baetican estates, including their housing, diet, punishments and reproduction.
entries.note: Columella, De Re Rustica (c.60 AD): 12 books. BnF and other libraries hold medieval manuscripts. His Gades origin: Columella I Praef.1. The chapters on enslaved workers: Book I.6–9 — 'the management of slaves' (primary documentation of agricultural slavery conditions). Ash (1941), Columella translation (Loeb): the standard English edition. The Baetican context: Columella draws on personal experience of Baetica — primary evidence for the province's agricultural organisation.

entries.label: Roman women in Hispanian inscriptions — the epigraphic evidence
entries.loc: Emerita Augusta; Tarraco; Barcino (main urban centres)
entries.hint: The Roman inscriptions of Hispania (over 20,000 surviving) include substantial numbers commemorating women — as wives, mothers, priestesses and occasionally in economic roles; the Hispanian inscriptions are among the richest evidence for Roman women's lives outside Rome and Italy.
entries.note: Hispanian inscriptions: EDCS (Epigraphik-Datenbank Clauss-Slaby) — digitised corpus of 20,000+ Hispanian inscriptions. The Emerita Augusta female priests (flamines): multiple inscriptions document women in religious office. Dixon (1992), The Roman Family: uses Hispanian inscriptions as evidence. The mosaics at Italica depicting women: primary visual evidence. Curchin (1991), Roman Spain ch.7 on social life.

events.l: Cantabrian Wars end — Hispania fully pacified
Flavian grant — Latin rights for all
Caracalla — full citizenship for all Hispani
Council of Elvira — first Western council
Priscillian — first Christian executed for heresy
Hosius of Corduba — Nicene Creed architect
Orosius — first Christian philosophy of history
Las Médulas — largest Roman gold mine
Via Augusta — logistical spine of Roman Hispania
Via Augusta completed — Cádiz to Pyrenees
